title: The Political Origins of Section 13(3) of the Federal Reserve Act

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This New York Fed press release announces a historical research article by Parinitha Sastry exploring the 1932 legislative origins of Section 13(3) of the Federal Reserve Act, which expanded the Fed's emergency lending authority to include nonbank institutions. The article concludes that the original framers intended to endow the Fed with the ability to lend directly to the real economy during emergencies. The analysis provides historical context for understanding the Fed's evolving role as an emergency liquidity provider during the 2008 financial crisis.
key_points: Section 13(3) was added at the height of the Great Depression in 1932
The provision expanded Fed lending authority to include individuals, partnerships, and corporations during emergencies
Article explores the political and legislative history of the original section
Author concludes the 1932 framers intended the Fed to lend directly to the real economy in emergencies
The analysis provides insights into the Fed's evolving role as an emergency liquidity provider
Authored by Parinitha Sastry, former senior research analyst at the New York Fed
why_it_matters: Understanding the historical legislative intent behind Section 13(3) provides context for debates about the Fed's emergency lending powers used during the 2008 financial crisis and ongoing discussions about central bank crisis-response frameworks.
macro_context: Section 13(3) was originally added in 1932 during the Great Depression and became the legal basis for the Fed's unprecedented emergency lending to nonbank institutions during the 2008 financial crisis. This historical research illuminates the original Congressional intent behind the provision.
market_structure_context: The article examines the legislative origins of the Fed's emergency lending authority—a key component of financial system infrastructure. While the analysis is historical rather than operational, it provides foundational context for understanding the Fed's role as an emergency liquidity provider to the broader financial system.
